inputs.story: A number of "real life superheroes" have set up shop in Paradise City, hoping to clean up the streets. Match each person to his superhero identity, and determine the year he started and the neighborhood he patrols. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas.
2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
inputs.constants:
superheroes: Deep Shadow; Green Avenger; Max Fusion; Ultra Hex.
years: 2007; 2008; 2009; 2010.
real names: Arnold Ashley; Lyle Lucas; Orel Osborne; Red Reilly.
inputs.solution:
superheroes	years	real names
deep shadow	2009	arnold ashley 
ultra hex	2008	lyle lucas 
green avenger	2010	orel osborne 
max fusion	2007	red reilly

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
A number of "real life superheroes" have set up shop in Paradise City, hoping to clean up the streets. Match each person to his superhero identity, and determine the year he started and the neighborhood he patrols. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas.
2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.

Constants:
superheroes: Deep Shadow; Green Avenger; Max Fusion; Ultra Hex.
years: 2007; 2008; 2009; 2010.
real names: Arnold Ashley; Lyle Lucas; Orel Osborne; Red Reilly.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Let's break down the problem to generate the ASP program.

**Problem Analysis:**
- Categories:
  - superheroes: Deep Shadow, Green Avenger, Max Fusion, Ultra Hex
  - years: 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010
  - real names: Arnold Ashley, Lyle Lucas, Orel Osborne, Red Reilly
- Clues:
  1. The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas.
     -> This implies these four are distinct entities. So:
        - year 2007 != Green Avenger
        - year 2007 != Deep Shadow
        - year 2007 != Lyle Lucas
        - Green Avenger != Deep Shadow
        - Green Avenger != Lyle Lucas
        - Deep Shadow != Lyle Lucas
  2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
     -> year(Arnold Ashley) = year(Ultra Hex) + 1
  3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
     -> year(Arnold Ashley) = 2009
  4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
     -> superhero(Green Avenger) = Orel Osborne (or they are the same person)

**Derivations from Clues:**
From 3: Arnold Ashley -> 2009.
From 2: Ultra Hex -> 2009 - 1 = 2008.
From 1: 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas are all different.
   Since Arnold Ashley is 2009, he is not in {2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas}.
   So Arnold Ashley's superhero is either Max Fusion or Ultra Hex. But Ultra Hex is 2008, so Arnold Ashley's superhero is Max Fusion.
   Wait, let's check carefully:
   Superheroes: Deep Shadow, Green Avenger, Max Fusion, Ultra Hex.
   Years: 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010.
   Real names: Arnold Ashley, Lyle Lucas, Orel Osborne, Red Reilly.
   From 3: Arnold Ashley = 2009.
   From 2: Ultra Hex = 2008.
   From 1: 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas are all different.
   So Green Avenger != 2007, Deep Shadow != 2007, Lyle Lucas != 2007.
   Green Avenger != Deep Shadow, Green Avenger != Lyle Lucas, Deep Shadow != Lyle Lucas.
   From 4: Green Avenger = Orel Osborne.
   So Orel Osborne = Green Avenger.
   Since Arnold Ashley = 2009, and Green Avenger = Orel Osborne, and Deep Shadow != Green Avenger, Deep Shadow != Orel Osborne.
   Lyle Lucas != Green Avenger, so Lyle Lucas != Orel Osborne.
   Who is 2007? It can't be Green Avenger/Orel Osborne, Deep Shadow, or Lyle Lucas. So it must be Red Reilly.
   So Red Reilly = 2007.
   Who is 2010? The remaining superhero is Max Fusion. So Max Fusion = 2010.
   Who is Deep Shadow? Superheroes left: Deep Shadow. Years left for names: 2007(Red), 2008(Ultra), 2009(Arnold), 2010(Max).
   Names left: Lyle Lucas, Orel Osborne(Green), Red Reilly(2007), Arnold Ashley(2009).
   Wait, let's list assignments:
   - Red Reilly -> 2007 -> Superhero? Not Green Avenger, not Deep Shadow, not Ultra Hex (2008). So Red Reilly = Max Fusion? Wait, Max Fusion is 2010.
   Let's re-evaluate carefully.
   We have 4 people. Each has a Real Name, Superhero, Year.
   Known:
   Arnold Ashley -> 2009
   Ultra Hex -> 2008
   From 1: 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas are distinct.
   So 2007 is not Green Avenger, not Deep Shadow, not Lyle Lucas.
   Therefore, 2007 is either Arnold Ashley, Ultra Hex, Max Fusion, or Red Reilly.
   But Arnold Ashley is 2009. Ultra Hex is 2008.
   So 2007 must be either Max Fusion or Red Reilly.
   From 1, Lyle Lucas is not 2007.
   Green Avenger is not 2007.
   Deep Shadow is not 2007.
   So 2007 is either Max Fusion or Red Reilly.
   Wait, the clue says "The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas." This implies these four refer to the four distinct individuals in the puzzle. So each of these four descriptions corresponds to exactly one person, and they are all different people.
   Thus:
   Person A: started in 2007
   Person B: Green Avenger
   Person C: Deep Shadow
   Person D: Lyle Lucas
   These are 4 distinct people.
   We know Arnold Ashley started in 2009. So Arnold Ashley is not A, B, C, or D.
   Therefore, Arnold Ashley's superhero is the one not listed in {Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Ultra Hex} -> Max Fusion.
   So Arnold Ashley = Max Fusion = 2009.
   We know Ultra Hex = 2008.
   So Person D (Lyle Lucas) cannot be Ultra Hex (2008) or Max Fusion (2009).
   Person D is either Person A (2007) or Person C (Deep Shadow)? But they are distinct. So Lyle Lucas is not Deep Shadow.
   Lyle Lucas is not Green Avenger.
   Lyle Lucas is not 2007.
   So Lyle Lucas must be Ultra Hex = 2008.
   So Lyle Lucas = Ultra Hex = 2008.
   Now we have:
   Arnold Ashley = Max Fusion = 2009
   Lyle Lucas = Ultra Hex = 2008
   Remaining Superheroes: Green Avenger, Deep Shadow.
   Remaining Years: 2007, 2010.
   Remaining Names: Orel Osborne, Red Reilly.
   From 4: Green Avenger = Orel Osborne.
   So Orel Osborne = Green Avenger.
   From 1: 2007 is distinct from Green Avenger. So Orel Osborne is not 2007.
   Therefore, Orel Osborne = Green Avenger = 2010.
   Then Red Reilly = Deep Shadow = 2007.
   Let's check consistency:
   1. 2007(Red), Green Avenger(Orel), Deep Shadow(Red), Lyle Lucas. All distinct? Yes.
   2. Arnold(2009) began 1 year after Ultra Hex(2008). Yes.
   3. 2009 is Arnold. Yes.
   4. Green Avenger is Orel. Yes.
   Solution:
   Red Reilly, Deep Shadow, 2007
   Lyle Lucas, Ultra Hex, 2008
   Arnold Ashley, Max Fusion, 2009
   Orel Osborne, Green Avenger, 2010

**ASP Encoding Strategy:**
- Categories: `superhero`, `year`, `real_name`.
- Predicate: `hero(real_name, superhero, year)`
- Constants:
  - superhero("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
  - year(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
  - real_name("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").
- Search space: `{hero(R, S, Y): superhero(S), year(Y)}=1 :- real_name(R).`
- Uniqueness: `{R1=R2; S1=S2; Y1=Y2}=0 :- hero(R1,S1,Y1), hero(R2,S2,Y2), (R1,S1,Y1)!=(R2,S2,Y2).`
- Clues:
  1. "The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas." -> They are all distinct.
     We can encode pairwise inequalities:
     `:- hero(R1,"Green Avenger",Y1), hero(R2,"Deep Shadow",Y2), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1,"Green Avenger",Y1), hero(R2,"Deep Shadow",Y2), Y1=Y2.`
     Actually, simpler: for each pair of descriptions, they cannot refer to the same person.
     Descriptions: `year=2007`, `superhero="Green Avenger"`, `superhero="Deep Shadow"`, `real_name="Lyle Lucas"`.
     We can just state that no person satisfies more than one of these conditions unless they are the same description? No, they are distinct people.
     So:
     `:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).`
     `:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).`
     `:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero(R, "Deep Shadow", Y2).` (Already covered by uniqueness on S)
     Actually, the standard way to encode "A, B, C, D are different" when they map to attributes is to enforce pairwise inequality on the person variable.
     Let's use explicit inequalities:
     `:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero(R2, "Deep Shadow", Y2), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero(R2, S, 2007), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero("Lyle Lucas", S, Y2), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1, "Deep Shadow", Y1), hero(R2, S, 2007), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1, "Deep Shadow", Y1), hero("Lyle Lucas", S, Y2), R1=R2.`
     `:- hero(R1, S, 2007), hero("Lyle Lucas", S2, Y2), R1=R2.`
     This is a bit verbose. A cleaner way in ASP for "A, B, C, D are distinct" where each is defined by a predicate/value:
     We can just state that no single person can have multiple of these attributes simultaneously.
     `:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).`
     `:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).`
     This covers all overlaps. Since we only have 4 people, and each has exactly one S and one Y, if two descriptions overlap on a person, it's forbidden. This is equivalent to saying they are distinct people.
     Wait, what if "Green Avenger" and "Deep Shadow" overlap? That's forbidden by uniqueness on superhero.
     So we only need to forbid overlaps between the specific values:
     `:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).`
     `:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).`
     `:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).`
     This is correct and concise.

  2. "Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex."
     `Y1 = Y2 - 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", S1, Y1), hero(R2, "Ultra Hex", Y2).`

  3. "The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley."
     `:- hero(R, S, 2009), R!="Arnold Ashley".`
     Or `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", S, Y).` (This forces Arnold to 2009, but doesn't forbid others. Better to use constraint or equality.)
     Actually, `:- hero(R, S, 2009), R != "Arnold Ashley".` is good.
     Or just `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).` and `:- hero(R, _, 2009), R != "Arnold Ashley".`

  4. "Green Avenger is Orel Osborne."
     `:- hero("Orel Osborne", S, Y), S != "Green Avenger".`
     `:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", Y), R != "Orel Osborne".`

Let's assemble and refine.

Constants:
```asp
superhero("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
year(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
real_name("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").
```

Search space:
```asp
{hero(R, S, Y): superhero(S), year(Y)}=1 :- real_name(R).
```

Uniqueness:
```asp
{R1=R2; S1=S2; Y1=Y2}=0 :- hero(R1,S1,Y1), hero(R2,S2,Y2), (R1,S1,Y1)!=(R2,S2,Y2).
```

Clues:
1. Distinctness of {2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas}:
```asp
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).
:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).
```
Wait, is it possible that "Lyle Lucas" is the same as "Green Avenger"? The clue says "The four people are...". This implies they are four distinct individuals. So yes, Lyle Lucas != Green Avenger, Lyle Lucas != Deep Shadow, Lyle Lucas != 2007.
The constraints above cover it.

2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex:
```asp
Y_arnold = Y_ultra + 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y_arnold), hero(_, "Ultra Hex", Y_ultra).
```

3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley:
```asp
:- hero(R, _, 2009), R != "Arnold Ashley".
```
(We can also add `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).` to be safe, but uniqueness + constraint is enough. Actually, adding `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).` is better.)

4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne:
```asp
:- hero("Orel Osborne", S, _), S != "Green Avenger".
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", _), R != "Orel Osborne".
```

Let's double check clue 1 encoding. Sometimes it's safer to use the pattern from examples:
`{C1; C2; C3; C4}=1 :- ...` but here they refer to attributes of different categories.
The pairwise inequality on the person variable is standard.
`:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero(R2, "Deep Shadow", Y2), R1=R2.`
`:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero(R2, S, 2007), R1=R2.`
`:- hero(R1, "Green Avenger", Y1), hero("Lyle Lucas", S, Y2), R1=R2.`
`:- hero(R1, "Deep Shadow", Y1), hero(R2, S, 2007), R1=R2.`
`:- hero(R1, "Deep Shadow", Y1), hero("Lyle Lucas", S, Y2), R1=R2.`
`:- hero(R1, S, 2007), hero("Lyle Lucas", S2, Y2), R1=R2.`
This is equivalent to my shorter constraints and more explicit. I'll stick with the shorter ones as they are logically equivalent and cleaner, but I will verify if any edge case exists.
If `hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007)` is false, then no one is both Green Avenger and 2007.
If `hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007)` is false, then no one is both Deep Shadow and 2007.
If `hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y)` is false, then Lyle is not Green Avenger.
If `hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y)` is false, then Lyle is not Deep Shadow.
If `hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007)` is false, then Lyle is not 2007.
This covers all 6 pairwise combinations of the 4 entities. It's correct.

Let's format the ASP program carefully.

```asp
% Constants
superhero("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
year(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
real_name("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").

% Search space
{hero(R, S, Y): superhero(S), year(Y)}=1 :- real_name(R).

% Uniqueness
{R1=R2; S1=S2; Y1=Y2}=0 :- hero(R1,S1,Y1), hero(R2,S2,Y2), (R1,S1,Y1)!=(R2,S2,Y2).

% 1. The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas.
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).
:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).

% 2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
Y_arnold = Y_ultra + 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y_arnold), hero(_, "Ultra Hex", Y_ultra).

% 3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).
:- hero(R, _, 2009), R != "Arnold Ashley".

% 4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
:- hero("Orel Osborne", S, _), S != "Green Avenger".
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", _), R != "Orel Osborne".

#show hero/3.
```

Wait, check clue 2 syntax: `Y_arnold = Y_ultra + 1 :- ...` is valid ASP arithmetic.
Check clue 3: `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).` is valid.
Check uniqueness rule: `{R1=R2; S1=S2; Y1=Y2}=0 :- hero(R1,S1,Y1), hero(R2,S2,Y2), (R1,S1,Y1)!=(R2,S2,Y2).`
This correctly enforces that no two distinct assignments share any category value. Actually, it enforces that if two assignments are different, they must differ in at least one category. Which is equivalent to saying each category value is used at most once. This matches the examples.

One minor point: In example 1, uniqueness is `{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).`
This is exactly what I have.

Let's verify the constraints for clue 1 again.
Is it possible that "Green Avenger" and "Deep Shadow" are the same person? Uniqueness on S prevents that.
Is it possible that "Green Avenger" and "Lyle Lucas" are the same? Covered.
Is it possible that "Deep Shadow" and "Lyle Lucas" are the same? Covered.
Is it possible that "2007" and "Green Avenger" are the same? Covered.
Is it possible that "2007" and "Deep Shadow" are the same? Covered.
Is it possible that "2007" and "Lyle Lucas" are the same? Covered.
All good.

One thing: `Y_arnold = Y_ultra + 1` might be better written as `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y1), hero(_, "Ultra Hex", Y2).` to avoid variable naming confusion, though it doesn't matter much. I'll stick to `Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y1), hero(_, "Ultra Hex", Y2).`

Also, for clue 3, `Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).` is fine.
For clue 4, `:- hero("Orel Osborne", S, _), S != "Green Avenger".` is fine.

```asp
% Constants
superhero("Deep Shadow"; "Green Avenger"; "Max Fusion"; "Ultra Hex").
year(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
real_name("Arnold Ashley"; "Lyle Lucas"; "Orel Osborne"; "Red Reilly").

% Search space
{hero(R, S, Y): superhero(S), year(Y)}=1 :- real_name(R).

% Uniqueness
{R1=R2; S1=S2; Y1=Y2}=0 :- hero(R1,S1,Y1), hero(R2,S2,Y2), (R1,S1,Y1)!=(R2,S2,Y2).

% 1. The four people are the person who started in 2007, Green Avenger, Deep Shadow and Lyle Lucas.
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", 2007).
:- hero(R, "Deep Shadow", 2007).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Green Avenger", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", "Deep Shadow", Y).
:- hero("Lyle Lucas", S, 2007).

% 2. Arnold Ashley began 1 year after Ultra Hex.
Y1 = Y2 + 1 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y1), hero(_, "Ultra Hex", Y2).

% 3. The person who started in 2009 is Arnold Ashley.
Y=2009 :- hero("Arnold Ashley", _, Y).
:- hero(R, _, 2009), R != "Arnold Ashley".

% 4. Green Avenger is Orel Osborne.
:- hero("Orel Osborne", S, _), S != "Green Avenger".
:- hero(R, "Green Avenger", _), R != "Orel Osborne".

#show hero/3.
```
